data.title: SK Telecom Is The Flight To Telecom Safety We Were Looking For
data.description:
Last week I wrote up an article about KT, the Korean Telecom company that I didn’t care for looking at the flight to safety. Their margin was falling, and they kept missing earnings estimates. So, then I got to thinking, if that’s not a good flight to safety telecom, what is?

Today we’ll be looking at one of KT’s competitors, SK Telecom Co., Ltd. (NYSE:SKM). It keeps showing up in my screeners as relatively cheap on a sector basis for telecom companies. Will it stack up as well as it looks initially? We will see.

SK Telecom is the largest mobile provider in South Korea, ahead of their main competitors KT and LG U+. SK Telecom holds about 40% of the market share, while each of the other two is in the range of 30% each (per the most recent 20-F). Though they continue to fare quite well, beating earnings estimates once again in 2024 (the third straight year they’ve done so), SK Telecom is also looking at revamping parts of their business to focus heavily on AI. We’ll be talking about their AI push and what it’s shaping up to look like, along with the numbers from the past fiscal year.

Earnings Surge in 2024 on Improved Operations

2023

2024

Operational Revenue

$12.4 billion

$12.6 billion

Operating Income

$1.23 billion

$1.28 billion

Net Income

$803 million

$1.01 billion

GAAP EPADS

$2.10

$2.64

(Source: February 6-K from SEC).

SK Telecom has been envisioning AI as the main driver of future revenue growth, but as you can see here, it hasn’t really started happening just yet. The company isn’t a grower, but they did have a massively improved net income number in 2024 because of a higher continuing operating income, which was up about 4% year over year.

The growth trend is expected to continue to be absent in 2025, and in the most recent conference call, SK Telecom said they expect revenue to be roughly flat, with some more improvements in operating income from further improvements in their operations.

2024 numbers translate to a GAAP P/E ratio of 8.41 at current prices, which is strongly better than the sector median of 18.44. This is a good start for the case that SK Telecom may be a strong value option.

Estimates for 2025 are revenue of $12.48 billion and non-GAAP EPADS of $2.84. Once again that’s quite a bit better than the sector, and this estimate might be on the low side, given that SK Telecom projected flat revenue, and further improved operating income. Given that, it’s hard to imagine the non-GAAP EPADS would fall from 2024’s number of $4.24 all the way to $2.84 on flat revenue. SK Telecom has beat estimates three straight years, and might well be shaping up to do it again.

The continued improvement of operating margins is obviously welcome, but what about revenue growth? For that, we’re going to have to take a close look at SK Telecom’s AI efforts because all indications are that’s where the growth will be coming from.

SK Telecom’s “AI Pyramid Strategy”

SK Telecom has designs on not just being a player in the global AI revolution, but one of the big ones. That may be ambitious, but they unveiled a plan called the Pyramid Strategy back in 2023 which gives us an idea of where they see this effort heading.

The company is now operating in two distinct sectors, mobile and AI, obviously with the former being a lot more lucrative than the latter right now. The hope is that will change, and if they can grow AI as they believe, it could well be something to watch. That doesn’t come free, though.

SK Telecom has said that they intend to triple the rate of investment in AI from the 2023 beginning of the strategy to 2028, up to KRW 25 trillion per year, which amounts to $17.6 billion US. These sectors aren’t totally independent either in the near term, with expectations that the AI sector will be able to strengthen their mobile and broadband offerings. It’s an AI world now, and it’s unsurprising SK Telecom is diving right in. They’re doing so in a big way too.

With regard to the B2C part of the business, they’re working on large-language model AI personal assistants. This includes a global goal of the GPAA or Global Personal AI Agent, but more significantly right now is “A.”.

A., or A dot as it is sometimes known, is an AI-powered personnel assistant capable of conversational interaction. The plan is to launch a paid version of A. later this year, and there’s a lot of excitement surrounding it. What’s out right now is considered quite impressive, and there’s big hope that it’s going to be the first big AI success story for the company. According to the conference call, they’ve already seen a 160% increase in A. users year-over-year, reflecting the momentum that’s starting to develop.

On the B2B side of things, they have a broad strategy called AIX, which aims to integrate AI advancements into business operations and expand cloud service. They have been partnering with multiple AI companies to that end.

The biggest effort on B2B so far is at the AI data center in Gasan, which is near Seoul. Under a partnership with Lambda Technologies, who SK Telecom has also invested in, they have substantially upgraded the Gasan data center, and have launched a GPU-as-a-service offering, which they hope will be embraced by various companies investing in AI development.

AI is an exciting topic, and SK Telecom is working with a lot of different companies to rise to the fore in it. It may be quite some time before this sector is profitable for them, but it shows promise, and they are investing big. Currently, the GPU-as-a-service offering and the A. paid edition are likely to be the focus in 2025, but there’s a constant effort to find different avenues of relevant growth here.

Dividends and Stock Buybacks, a High Return Stock

Dividends are a big part of the allure of telecom companies, and that’s a big part of why they’re traditionally thought of as a flight-to-safety choice in economic uncertainty. Even KT, which as mentioned before I didn’t really care for, has nearly a 4% yield, roughly in line with the sector median. SK Telecom looks even better in this regard.

They make their dividend payments in KRW, so it varies somewhat depending on the conversion rate. In general, they pay around 33¢ per quarter per ADS. This translates to a yield of 5.9%, quite a bit above the sector median. Then there’s also the possibility of stock repurchases in the future.

In their conference call, SK Telecom said the priority is to maintain dividend stability, but that they may also enter into share repurchase programs if stock trends warrant it. Currently, their price/book ratio is 1.02, better than the sector’s 1.82, so the buyback wouldn’t be a terrible idea. Obviously, cash needs to be preserved to invest in AI growth, but if they’re able, a buyback would be a great way to enhance returns for investors. Even if they don’t, the well above average dividend yield is already a reason to find this appealing.

South Korea’s Economy and the Risks of Going All-in on AI

Risk-wise, SK Telecom is not looking that dissimilar from KT, though without the problem of falling margins or repeated earnings misses. The big concern, as with everyone else, is what is to come of the global economy in the near term with a growing tariff war.

Now much like KT, SK Telecom isn’t a big export company or anything, but exports are quite important to the South Korean economy, and a recession would be a problem for virtually everyone. Though SK Telecom’s stock price might benefit from the perceived flight to safety factor, their earnings might be challenged if the whole economy is suffering.

Then there’s the AI factor. As mentioned before, SK Telecom is investing heavily in AI, and while there’s a lot of hope they’re making some progress in that end, we still don’t know what the future of AI will look like, nor if heavily investing in it right now will pay off in the long run. I don’t blame them for making such an effort to try to restart company revenue growth, but we should understand it comes with risks.

The Right Flight to Safety Stock

In my view, SK Telecom is a much, much better than KT as a flight-to-safety stock, and its numbers suggest there is a strong case to be made for it to be an income stock or a value stock. Though there are a lot of questions remaining about how the investment in AI will pan out, even that is looking encouraging so far, though it may well be years before we see real returns on that investment. I’m rating this a strong buy.

If you’re looking for a telecom stock or exposure in South Korea, you could do a lot worse than SK Telecom. Adding to the appeal, it isn’t trading near a 52-week high like a lot of stocks that have already experienced a flight-to-safety surge. SK Telecom is trading around the middle of its 52-week range.

Investors should keep a close eye on the GPU-as-a-service business in future quarters because that seems like it’s the most likely part of their AI sector to take off early. Watching progress on AI is also important because if it continues to grow the way it did in 2024, they could really have something there too.

data.title: MPLX Is A Strong Income Investment With Large Distributions And Growth
data.description:
The recent volatility has created an opportunity for investors with cash on the sidelines. Many companies have experienced significant retracements and income investments, such as MPLX LP (NYSE:MPLX), which are trading at enticing yields. MPLX has built an energy infrastructure company that connects several basins to the major markets in addition to terminals, refineries, and export facilities. As the demand for energy grows, MPLX is likely to experience increased levels of Adjusted EBITDA and Distributable Cash Flow (DCF), which will allow them to allocate more capital toward growth projects and increase the distributions going forward. MPLX is yielding close to 8% as its distribution is paying unitholders $3.83. The United States remains the largest producer of oil globally, and the upstream production of oil and gas will remain a critical component of the global economy as more individuals increase their standard of living and the age of artificial intelligence and robotics advances. I am looking at income investments with protected moats, and energy infrastructure is at the top of the list. Anyone can raise capital and try to compete in the software space, but you can't raise capital and decide to build pipelines. MPLX is an energy tollbooth, and I believe it's trading at a discount. Investors are able to lock in strong yields at these levels, with future distribution growth on the horizon.

Following up on my previous article about MPLX

Since I turned bullish on MPLX last July, shares have increased by 19.05%. Since the last article that I wrote in the middle of January (can be read here) MPLX has mitigated downside risk better than the market as its units have declined by -2.18% while the S&P 500 has declined by -10.26%. The total return for MPLX has been -0.34% since then, after the distribution is factored in. In that article, I discussed why I was bullish after Q3 earnings, and how I believed the macroeconomic landscape was setting up well for MPLX. Now that the 2024 fiscal year is over, I am following up with a new article, as I believe MPLX is in a strong position to benefit from increased revenue and profitability as the demand for energy continues to grow. Now that we have the data from the mega caps around CapEx spending and chip production, I believe that the bull case for MPLX is stronger than ever. With the expansion expected to occur, more traditional energy sources will be needed, and MPLX will be a clear beneficiary as the years progress.

Risks to investing in MPLX

Just because I am very bullish on MPLX and other energy infrastructure companies, that doesn't mean that these investment ideas will work out how I envision they will. Upstream production in the United States could stall and then decline, which would directly impact MPLX as less fuel would be moving through their system. If the demand for energy declines, then MPLX will have trouble contracting its pipeline capacity in the future. If the advancements in technology plateau, then there could be a slowdown in CapEx spending as the mega caps will no longer be in competition for the latest GPUs as they focus more on utilizing their current infrastructure. If this occurs, the projected forecasts for the demand of energy would likely decline, and MPLX would be directly impacted. MPLX also faces operational risks, and if they experience pipeline leaks, it could lead to unprecedented environmental damage, which could become costly from both a capital and reputation standpoint. MPLX could also underperform the market, as it has over the past 5-10 years. If the story around the demand for energy changes, MPLX may not be able to grow its distribution at the same pace and could be forced to cut the distribution at some point. Investors should do their own research, as investing in MPLX is not a sure bet.

Tax information

MPLX is a limited partnership that issues a Schedule K-1 tax package. These are normally mailed to their unitholders in mid-March. Anyone who is a current unitholder or interested in investing in MPLX should read through the MPLX Tax Reporting Package (can be found here) and discuss with a tax professional how this may impact your taxes.

MPLX is an income investor's dream, and the current valuation presents a strong opportunity

Income investors typically look for high-quality yields rather than outsized returns. It's a different mindset as they are focused on recurring income that is growing rather than trying to match or beat the market. There are two schools of thought regarding income investing: some investors look for large yields, while others look for annualized growth. MPLX offers something of both as the current yield is almost 8%, and MPLX has increased its distribution for the past 11 years. MPLX offers a high-quality distribution, as it's protected by fee-based contracts from commodities that are essential to the economy. MPLX has been able to grow its distribution through recessions and the pandemic, while others had to cut or stop their annualized increases. I believe that MPLX is likely to continue increasing the distribution as they continue to grow their cash from operations and bring more projects online to facilitate the increased demand for their services.

Since going public at the end of 2012, MPLX has generated a tremendous amount of income for its investors. At the end of 2012, MPLX units traded for $32.25 and generated $30.08 in distributed income. The quarterly distribution has grown by 440.70% from $0.18 to $0.96. Over the past 5-years, MPLX has increased its distribution at an average of 6.08%, and its latest increase took the quarterly distribution from $0.85 to $0.96. This is a high-quality distribution that has allowed investors to benefit from both the amount of income it produces and the annualized growth.

The latest CPI report came in at 2.4%, as inflation has fallen from 3.5% over the past year. Unemployment has gone the opposite way, as it has increased from 3.9% to 4.2% over this period. CME Group is expecting the Fed to cut at least 3 times in 2025, with more than a 40% chance that the Fed cuts at least 4 times. In an environment where the Fed is cutting and the risk-free rate of return declines, the distribution yield from MPLX will look more enticing to income investors because the base yield will double the risk-free rate of return with historical annualized increases. I think there is a strong opportunity for income investors with units of MPLX trading for around $50, especially since they have been bringing projects online to drive higher amounts of cash flow from their operations.

MPLX posted a strong 2024 fiscal year, and 2025 has a lot of potential to see increases across the board

MPLX made a lot of progress in the 2024 fiscal year. MPLX finished Q4, generating $1.1 billion in net income and $1.48 billion in DCF. This allowed MPLX to have a distribution coverage ratio of 1.5x during Q4 based on the quarterly distribution of $0.96. In the 2024 fiscal year, MPLX was a cash flow machine, which generated $5.9 billion in cash from operations and $5.7 billion in distributable cash flow. MPLX's revenue increased 4.21% ($450 million) to $11.13 billion and produced $6.33 billion in gross profit, which was an increase of 5.05% ($304 million). MPLX is operating a high-margin business with a 56.84% gross profit margin and large margins on the other profitability metrics. MPLX's EBITDA increased 4.64% YoY to $5.77 billion at a margin of 56.84%. MPLX generated $4.89 billion in FCF during 2024, which was a YoY increase of 9.64% ($430 million), while its net income grew 9.9% ($389 million) to $4.32 billion. MPLX was able to produce a 43.93% FCF margin and a 38.78% profit margin. This is impactful based on the operations that came online and the pipeline of new projects funded by their retained DCF.

I'm excited about MPLX's growth strategy, as it's expected to allocate $1.7 billion toward new projects that are expected to come online in 2025. The Gulf Coast Fractionation Complex will be in partnership with ONEOK (OKE). This project will strengthen the NGL value chain for MPLX by increasing fractionation capacity and its export capabilities. MPLX has been diversifying its revenue mix into natural gas and natural gas liquids (NGL) services, and this endeavor will add to its diversification. The BANGL pipeline expansion is expected to boost its capacity to 250,000 bpd of NGLs from the Permian Basin. The expansion is expected to be completed and brought online in Q1, 2025. The last project I am excited about this year is the Secretariat Processing Plant, which will increase MPLX's gathering and processing segment. This will be MPLX's 7th plant in the Permian. These projects show MPLX's commitment to its growth strategy and diversifying its revenue mix away from crude. MPLX has grown its DCF by 6.9% CAGR since the close of 2021, adding $1.1 billion in DCF. This has allowed them to grow the distribution at a 10.7% CAGR since the 2021 fiscal year, as the distribution has grown by 10% or more in each of the last 3 fiscal years.

When I look at the EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook, the total production and consumption of liquid fuels is expected to increase through 2026. The EIA is also expecting that the amount of energy consumption in 2025 and 2026 will increase compared to prior years. This is mainly due to the number of data centers coming online, and MPLX is in a great position. As the demand for energy increases, the projects MPLX is bringing online should be instantly accreditive to earnings, and we could have several more years of continued top and bottom-line growth in the financials. MPLX provides critical infrastructure, and based on the EIA projections, there should be more than enough demand for the operations that come into production from MPLX in 2025.

Conclusion

I think there will be a lot of opportunities for MPLX in the coming years. As the demand for energy continues to grow, it should provide MPLX with the ability to drive larger amounts of cash flow from its operations. MPLX has been an income investor's dream, and investors now have another opportunity to add shares at around the $50 level. MPLX is yielding around 8% with a distribution growth rate that exceeds a 6% CAGR over the past 5-years and a 10% CAGR over the last 3-years. MPLX could be in a position to continue growing the distribution at these rates, and in an environment where the Fed will be cutting the large yield, it will look very enticing compared to the deteriorating risk-free rate of return. MPLX is operating a high-margin energy infrastructure business, and more than 30% of every dollar of additional revenue generated goes to the bottom line. I expect MPLX units to rally throughout the year, and we will likely see another large increase in the distribution in November.

data.title: Target Is A Value Target
data.description:
The investment thesis for Target Corporation (NYSE:TGT) is very strong for a value investor. Contra positions can be considered at a time, when the value outshines the risks to the thesis (and there are a few). Let us see why.

Several Key Indicators are Looking Great

Gross profit margins have been around the just sub 30% mark, which is great. The RoE is standout – at close to 27%. Free Cash Flow is also looking healthy – one of the healthiest among retailers for its revenue size. At $3.7b, the levered free cash flow for 2024, is significantly higher than the $2.3b reported in 2023. This is a continued improvement after the cash flow impact in 2022, where TGT was hit on discretionary spending – stocked up inventory anticipating strong demand continuation post-COVID. This was also a period of heavy capex investment by TGT on revamping infrastructure and going bigger on ecommerce fulfillment.

Some may argue that these are lagging indicators – what matters is how TGT will react to the likely upcoming drop in discretionary spending and rising prices due to tariffs. Firstly, TGT’s similar experience not so far back in 2022-23 should hold them in good stead this time round. Even in the case of a deeper and wider impact this time, look at two things to derive comfort. The prices have already corrected anticipating this downturn (sure, they could correct even further, but unlike in 2022, TGT's share price seems more prepared). Secondly, valuation. At a forward PE ratio of 10.21, TGT is trading at a deep discount in comparison to its own 5-year historical average of 18.58 (and a sector median of ~18). These factors were not the same in 2022-23. TGT has braced for impact. It is very possible there will be an impact, but TGT may be better positioned to ride through it.

TGT has a high dividend yield of 4.83%, great for cushioning the ride as you hold TGT for the valuation multiple expansion from an economic rebound (or simply valuation adjustment on threat reassessment). The dividend was not impacted in the last stress period of 2022-23 (stress for TGT) and the dividend outlook remains good for the upcoming years, as the strong free cash flow history suggests decent financial stability to fund dividends.

Risks to the Thesis

One, an overall economic downturn that impacts retail across the board. Two, a hit on discretionary spending impacts TGT more than essential retailers and competitors. Three, Amazon and Walmart’s competitive pressures along with discount retailers such as Costco. Four, political and social boycotts have likely been impacting TGT sales in the recent past. And Five, tariff impacts on supply chain disruptions and rising prices to be passed on to customers (or eating into the margins).

Major headwinds those! On one and two, recent learnings, strategy, and the bounce back from the lows of 2022-23 should prove valuable for TGT. WMT and AMZN did not go through the same levels of stress as TGT, and I expect that to reflect in how TGT reacts. Three is real – particularly shifts to discount retailers or non-discretionary offerings are a TGT-specific threat compared to competition. Four and Five are not specific to TGT. Five, in particular, is a two-edged sword. While all retailers are reviewing contracts and realigning strategic supply chains, there is a possibility that the markets may have assumed and priced in the worst. Any positive developments could set a reversal in action (although even the temporary damage is bad enough).

The major question is how much of the risks, particularly the ones specific to TGT’s discretionary offering are already factored in. We cannot time the markets and don’t know the exact extent of the tariff crisis or its play-out impact time. I am not underestimating it at all – developments and repercussions are huge at face value. All I am saying is that, value investing is often a contra bet – waiting for the rebound may mean missing the bus. I would not argue the same to be true for other stocks that have not shown the same levels of valuation discounts (and yes, I know that discount is for a reason – a strong one). What tilts me to value buy TGT, at least from a long-term lens, is the correction already in place. TGT is trading at around 5-year low levels – it is hard to ignore a Buy call at this juncture on valuation. Fundamentals are intact, dividends are in play.

Long Term Target

A worrying trend for TGT has been stagnation in the growth of revenues and its absence from global markets – unlike its peers Walmart and Amazon. So what is TGT doing to address long-term prospects?

Target Plus Marketplace – the third-party digital, is working on a strategy to include new brands and attract a stronger customer base with aims to grow the business five-fold over the next few years. Investments are also in place to boost digital revenue streams from Roundel. Store expansion and remodeling are cushioned by the huge free cash flow (and debt – TGT has a high debt-to-equity ratio, even for a physical retailer, but not concerning levels). Some investments were already made in the stress period of 2022-23 when Same Day Deliveries were strengthened (Shipt acquisition in 2017 and stepping up the drive in 2022-23). AI and technology to improve the customer experience is also in. All the usual suspects for a physical retailer! How TGT executes those plans – been good so far – will be key.

Why I Like TGT

Other than valuations, dividends, and strong financials, I see a quiet technology revolution in action. TGT is already using AI for not only the customer experience but also for internal training, supply chain optimizations, and pricing. Their tech stack is completely owned in-house – no reliance on third parties. I also see TGT as a brand incubator – not just retail. Brand launches specifically targeting when there's a wider downturn in discretionary is not a difficult ask for TGT.

Anything below $95 is a buy for TGT, for a target of $150. Dividends are expected to be in the vicinity of 4.5%. Don’t miss this contra opportunity!

data.title: TG Therapeutics: Progress In MS Tempered By High Expectations
data.description:
Overview

Multiple sclerosis (MS) drug developer, TG Therapeutics (NASDAQ:TGTX) is up another 25% since my rating downgrade in January. While the firm’s intravenous anti-CD20 therapy, Briumvi (ublituximab), was carving a niche in the competitive MS market, its plans for a subcutaneous version seemed late to the punch. So, my Hold rating was really a balancing act between short-term gains and long-term challenges.

The company has since reported its full year 2024 earnings, so I figure this is a good time to reevaluate.

Recent Developments

In Q4 and FY24, Briumvi generated $103.6 million and $310 million in revenue, respectively. So, Briumvi is still accelerating following its January 2023 launch. As for 2025, TGTX guided Briumvi revenues to be “approximately $540 million.” Meanwhile, Briumvi launched in the EU. Its partner, Neuraxpharm, spearheads marketing efforts.

TGTX continues to strengthen Briumvi’s clinical data and administration convenience. On the former, open-label five-year data revealed that 92% of Briumvi patients were “free from disability progression.” Regarding administration convenience, 30-minute Briumvi infusions were well tolerated in a Phase 3b trial. Currently, Briumvi is administered over an hour, which compares favorably over competitors like Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) Ocrevus (2-4 hours).

Recently, TGTX began a Phase 1 trial evaluating subcutaneous ublituximab in patients with relapsing forms of MS. So, TGTX is aiming to compete directly with Novartis’ (NVS) blockbuster Kesimpta. This is smart because subcutaneous solutions are growing in popularity in the MS market due to convenience. Despite being approved nearly five years ago, Kesimpta is still seeing massive growth. It generated $3.224 billion in 2024 (up 49% year-over-year). Currently, Kesimpta can be administered at home via an auto-injector pen. Patients are instructed to take it weekly for the first three weeks, skip a week, and then administer it monthly. In September 2024, Roche, too, entered the subcutaneous market with the approval of Ocrevus Zunovo. However, Ocrevus Zunovo requires an office visit twice a year for its 10-minute administration.

TGTX believes subcutaneous ublituximab's efficacy can hold up if administered every other month. This would offer a convenience advantage over Kesimpta. On the flip side, TGTX may come across a number of barriers. For starters, ublituximab’s efficacy will have to match or exceed Kesimpta’s despite less frequent administration. If approved, ublituximab then needs to overcome Kesimplta’s significant first-mover advantage (i.e. established prescriber familiarity and patient experience). Notably, Novartis is, too, building the case for Kesimpta with six year data demonstrating its efficacy and safety in first-line and switch patients.

TGTX is also exploring other autoimmune applications for subcutaneous ublituximab, like myasthenia gravis (MG). This is in Phase 1 development. Anti-CD20 utilization is not new for MG. There is evidence that rituximab, another anti-CD20, is effective in select MG patients (MuSK-positive), but its off-label use is limited to refractory cases. Notably, it has been estimated that only 5-8% of MG patients are MuSK-positive.

TGTX has been working to expand its product offerings beyond ublituximab. With its partner, Precision BioSciences, it is evaluating azer-cel, an allogeneic CD19 CAR T therapy, for treating MS. This is in Phase 1 development. This is a very speculative venture that reflects a broader industry trend of evaluating cell therapies in autoimmune conditions.

Financial Health

Taking a closer look at Q4 earnings, total revenues were $108.2 million. Cost of revenue was $15.8 million. SG&A and R&D expenses totaled $38.963 million and $23.874 million, respectively. Net income for Q4 was $23.33 million, compared to a net loss of $14.416 million in Q4 2023. Notably, the rate of expense growth (+35% year-over-year) was outpaced by total revenue growth (+41%, excluding the impact of one-time license in 2023), highlighting Briumvi’s adoption and the company’s operating leverage.

As of December 31, TGTX held $311 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investment securities. It owes $244.429 million in loan payable, but maintains total stockholders’ equity of $222.364 million.

Valuation Assumptions

I ran a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis to get an idea about the expectations implied by TGTX’s valuation. As for inputs, I felt 20% revenue growth and 25% free cash flow margins were reasonable long-term goals. Note that TGTX had a negative FCF margin in 2024. 25%, although reasonable, is an optimistic assumption based on historical financial numbers for mature pharmaceutical companies. 20% revenue growth would exceed MS market size CAGR of 7.9%. Please note that 20% represents an “average” over the nine-year projection period. In reality, TGTX’s growth will not be linear.

So, 20% growth assumes that TGTX is taking a lot of market share from its competitors. “Cost of debt” and “tax rate” are estimates (actual figures depend on market conditions, the company's performance, etc.) based on available information (10-K) and do not have much impact on its overall discount rate as TGTX is predominately equity-based. The stock beta of 1.5 (over the past 24 months) is derived from Seeking Alpha data. Some sources have TGTX beta as being higher (depending on the timeline). The terminal growth % of 2.5 is a standard model assumption. For a company with one product so far, it is optimistic to assume it will have any terminal growth beyond the projection period.

Even based on optimistic assumptions, TGTX is overvalued by nearly 30%.

Next, I conduct a sensitivity analysis to assess how key changes to inputs (revenue growth and FCF margins) impact valuation. This also acts as a “reverse DCF model.”

TGTX appears to be priced for 26.4% revenue growth (assuming 25% FCF margins).

Finally, my Monte Carlo simulation accounts for variation among key inputs like growth, margins, beta, and discount rate to provide a more dynamic and realistic view of share price outcomes.

Again, these are based on my assumptions and garbage in = garbage out when it comes to financial models.

The key takeaway is this: TGTX is already priced for a lot of success (>25% year-over-year revenue growth and FCF margins).

Conclusion

To wrap things up, TGTX’s Briumvi is outperforming internal and analyst expectations in the competitive MS market. Granted, much of this is accounted for in its valuation, which has jumped over 170% within the past year.

Importantly, biologics have the advantage of 12-year market exclusivity and their prices do not suffer much (relative to small molecules) when biosimilars are introduced. This gives TGTX a lot of time to benefit from Briumvi revenues. Meanwhile, evaluating ublituximab as a subcutaneous injection follows market trends and may confer more years of market exclusivity. Reaching profitability has also allowed TGTX to comfortably expand beyond just ublituximab in MS. Efforts in other autoimmune conditions (e.g., MG) and treatment modalities (e.g., cell therapy) allow for optionality and aim to expand its position in the large and growing autoimmune market. That being said, per reverse DCF, TGTX appears reasonably priced at the moment, meriting a rating of Hold and market perform.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ASML Is Quietly Preparing For A Post-Tariff Boom, Are You? - Buy Call
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last time I covered ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML), I gave it a solid buy rating. Then, they had a positive outlook for the rest of 2025 and a growing monopoly in the EUV market, both of which they still have. I anticipated that they would continue with a positive outlook for 1Q25, and that's precisely what they’ve done. The problem for ASML is with the numbers for the current quarter. Even though they posted the highest gross margins in over a year at 54%, they missed on net bookings. This quarter saw a 16.4% revenue decline compared to 1Q24; there was a big drop-off in high-NA EUV system recognitions. Why the big drop?

As usual, the answer is China, whose cut of ASML’s total revenue dropped from 47% to 27%. Analysts are predicting this number to fall even further by the end of the year. Tariffs and geopolitical tensions are dominating the headlines, and this is seen clearest in their outlook on demand trends. Net bookings totaling €3.9 billion missed the consensus of €4.6 billion, giving investors the biggest scare, and the stock tumbled 6.3% in pre-market trading after the earnings release and is now down 6% since the market opened.

So, should investors be worried about ASML’s future? I’m here to tell you they shouldn’t and that this recent slip-up in bookings is just short-term collateral damage from Trump's trade war. If anything, this recent stock drop should be considered a good discount on ASML shares, giving investors a nice opening into the stock.

I don't expect this tariff war to continue, and it seems neither does ASML, as they maintained their healthy outlook for the rest of 2025 at €30B to €35B for the fiscal year. I reiterate my buy rating on ASML as this reduction in China sales won't last long.

The Rundown

To give investors the rundown, ASML missed Wall Street’s expectations this quarter, reporting €3.9 billion, about €600 million shy of consensus. This marked a pretty big 45% drop from €7.09 billion in 4Q24. Bookings for EUV tech took a €1.2 billion tumble, and investors got concerned that the overall orders for advanced process nodes may be dwindling due to tariff panic. Their EUV product lineup also got a bit more diverse with the inclusion of the NXE:3800 now adding to the revenue. ASML’s dividend share for 2024 was priced at €6.4 per share, a small 4.9% increase over the previous year. They also bought back €2.7 billion in shares in this first quarter. I think these are positive forward-looking signs about where the stock is headed.

A China Turnaround on the Horizon

In my previous article, I tracked the percentage of China's sales and showcased the steep decline in their export percentage. China accounted for just 27% of exports in 1Q25, following the same trend we saw at the end of last year. I’ve updated my previous graph below to include the most recent earnings results.

What we know is that ASML’s EUV technology has never been sold to China, so the tech exported to China must mainly be made up of DUV tools. That's precisely why EUV makes up 56% of their revenue this quarter, the highest ever for ASML. Just like I anticipated, EUV sales are trying their best to offset DUV shipments to China. ASML is having difficulty navigating headwinds into the tariffs, there's a US-China trade war, far tighter US export regulations on semiconductor tools, and tough investment rules being pushed on them from the EU.

However, in the long run, I do not expect this low China export trend to continue. The news is out today that China is already open to making a deal with the U.S., China’s leader has just expressed their need for "more respect" from the U.S. To reiterate my thesis, I don't see the current China tariffs lasting long; the current ASML selloff should turn around pretty quickly due to the demand for EUV technology going nowhere.

I believe investors should see this current decline in ASML stock as an entry point at a decent discount. The important bit of info here is that ASML still holds its positive outlook for the rest of 2025. That can only mean they anticipate China sales to return to normal levels, or in other words, for the tariffs to be lifted. All signs really point to this outcome; other countries have made deals with the US, and China will follow, but they’ll be the last to do so. Once a solid trade deal is made between the two countries, I expect orders of DUV tech to China to significantly increase. The global trading war has left the consumer uncertain, so we may see a brief stage of panic orders on DUV to China, which should offset ASML’s current miss in earnings.

Valuation

After the latest selloff, ASML holds a market cap of €226 billion. The stock price is at $652, down from $690 before the earnings report. Forward price-to-sales ratios are at 7.31, well above the sector mean of 2.39, while the forward EV-to-sales ratio is at 7.07 compared to the median of 2.4. However, I wouldn't follow SA’s evaluation too closely as they tend to pile a lot of names into that sector, while in reality, very few can actually do what ASML does.

What's Next?

ASML stock might have a bit further to fall before bottoming out completely. Investors wanting to buy in should be aware of this and time their entry point accordingly. The stock price is hovering around $644. In my previous article, the stock was priced at $716; since then, it has fallen quite a bit, but I'm not concerned. I think it's a good thing that market panic is getting priced in because it'll make for better windows to jump in for long-term investors.

Investors shouldn't wait too long, though, as any news that hints at China making a trade deal with the U.S. will surely boost the stock. ASML doesn’t appear to be worried enough about 2025 since they maintained their outlook, and I don't think investors should be, either.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BNDX: International Bond Diversification Doesn't Seem Worth It
data.description:
Are you wondering whether it's worth diversifying your U.S. Treasury portfolio? Well, if so, then you've come to the right place. This analysis covers the Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ:BNDX), a globally diversified bond vehicle with multi-sector exposure.

While we remain positive about U.S. treasuries, we acknowledge that some investors might seek external diversification, prompting us to communicate our thoughts of BNDX; here are a few things to consider.

Fund Overview

The Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF Shares tracks the Bloomberg Global Aggregate ex-USD Float Adjusted RIC Capped Index while hedging USD risk, essentially meaning investors gain exposure to major markets without facing USD risk exposure. Furthermore, the vehicle's effective duration is 7.1 years and the issuer diversified. Though its duration may change with time, BNDX is passively managed, meaning abrupt short-term shifts are unlikely.

The ETF's primary exposure is Japan, spanning over 12% of its portfolio. In addition, BNDX holds substantial exposure to France, Germany, Italy, the UK, Canada, and Spain—the diagram below illustrates BNDX's point-in-time exposure.

A critical note is BNDX's sector exposure. The vehicle doesn't isolate sovereign bonds. In fact, it invests throughout the credit hierarchy, possessing exposure to sovereign, corporate and asset-backed securities.

Here's a compact view of the ETF's credit exposure, supplementing the above-mentioned. Even though it emphasizes investment-grade credit (above BBB), BNDX doesn't isolate AAA-rated debt and invests in sub-grade debt.

The vehicle's historical performance echoes opportunity. BNDX has outperformed IEF ETF (IEF) in the past decade. Sure, IEF is an intermediate term U.S. Treasury pure play. Nevertheless, the comparison presents a telling relative view to those wondering about excess returns.

Aside: Risk premiums are mentioned later in the report.

Market Commentary

BNDX The Past Week

As many might know, U.S. Treasuries sold across the board last week. The event marked an unlikely outcome, as many anticipated treasuries to rise alongside fears of heightened recession risk. We remain upbeat about U.S. treasuries, yet think a relative comparison to BNDX communicates critical talking points. For example, BNDX holding up during a U.S. bond 'fire sale' suggests it might possess relative advantages in today's climate; is a relative advantage really in store? Let's find out.

Sovereign Spreads and CDS

Sovereign spreads, which measure the difference between a nation's 10-year yield versus the U.S. 10-year, often paint a valuable risk-adjusted return picture. According to our calculations, most of BNDX's key geographic areas have either faced spread tightening or additional weakening in recent years. Simultaneously, credit default swap spreads (agile measures of sovereign risk) have increased, suggesting higher absolute risk levels. In essence, quantitative measures indicate that most of BNDX's salient regions face receding risk-adjusted return prospects. The data suggests that opportunities remain in the U.K., but broad-based relative value still favours the United States.

Here's a view of the current environment. Please note the following: Swaps trade in a dark market, and intraday sovereign yields are volatile. As such, the numbers can differ slightly among vendors and analysts.

Country Sovereign Spread (Circa) 5-Y CDS (Circa)
Japan -3.01% 21.28 bps
France -0.93% 40.33 bps
Germany -1.51% 14.47 bps
UK 0.26% 22.09 bps
Canada -1.02% 39.60 bps
Italy -0.24% 61.67 bps

Source: Author's Calculations. Data: Trading Economics, World Government Bonds - Date: 07/04/2025 (Intraday)

This chart complements the argument by showing data from April 2022.

Country Sovereign Spread (Circa) 5-Y CDS (Circa)
Japan -1.58% 18.60 bps
France -1.41% 28.27 bps
Germany -1.75% 13.44 bps
UK -0.52% 12.76 bps
Canada 0.41% 32.34 bps
Italy 0.35% 100.14 bps

Source: Author's Calculations. Data: Trading Economics, World Government Bonds - Date: 04/04/2022 (Intraday)

Does qualitative data paint the full picture? No, let's examine qualitative events to extend the analysis.

Qualitative Tailwinds

  • Leading investment funds, including PIMCO have suggested that foreign diversification presents an opportunity in today's market environment. Institutional re-alignment can provide a qualitative base case to a pivot into foreign debt.
  • The U.K.'s economy is outperforming expectations in tandem with its positive sovereign spread. Collectively, these measures suggest an excess return opportunity has presented itself.

  • The EU recently announced intent to increase defence spending. Higher defence spending can rally interim GDP growth, which might be priced by the bond market in due course.
  • Germany has hinted at an attempt to become a capital importer under its new coalition government through the release of its debt brake and higher productivity spending.
  • The “basis trade” and "foreign adversaries selling U.S. treasuries" debacle is gathering steam. We think the narrative is overplayed, as re-location of U.S. debt investments from capital-exporting countries would be difficult to achieve. Moreover, a myriad of reasons could explain why treasuries sold aggressively in recent trading sessions; a basis trade argument is stale. Nonetheless, narrative often drives markets, and we think certain investors might re-locate their U.S. Treasury investments due to the prevailing sentiment of media and popular culture.

Qualitative Headwinds

  • France's enhanced sovereign spread should be placed into context. Fitch recently affirmed its AA- negative outlook, suggesting a spike in yields is warranted instead of presenting a value opportunity.
  • Germany's plans for fiscal reforms can be challenged by opposing parties. The country is in a political gridlock, presenting decision-making obstacles.
  • Much of Japan's economy relies on exports. Although tariff suspensions might provide interim benefits, a gradual decay of global trade confidence seems likely, paving the way for long-term headwinds.
  • The U.K.'s February GDP growth is an isolated event. The region has yet to deliver sustainable data to suggest it's exiting a trying economic period.

Credit Risk Outlook

Global corporate defaults experienced activity in 2023 and 2024, with speculative grade credit defaults rising. At the other end, investment-grade defaults remained relatively stable. While we expect deterioration in credit risk for the remainder of 2025, we think high-quality investment grade credits are safeguarded for now, unless severe tail risk surfaces on a global macro level (Think GFC, Covid, and not 2022/23).

Japan, which is BNDX's largest constituent, experienced docile re-ratings in asset-backed securities during 2024, likely mirrored in the corporate scene. In our view, the global trade war might pressure certain export companies, but we don't expect investment-grade entities to crumble just yet. Still, subprime debt might feel the brunt if deterioration in global economic sentiment resumes.

In essence, higher Probabilities of Default and Loss Given Default figures are possible for 2025. However, we see the phenomenon being non-investment grade-centric.

BNDX's Key Risk-Return Metrics

Yields

At the time of writing the article, BNDX had a 30-day SEC yield of 3.14%, which isn't particularly high, considering IEF ETF's 30-day SEC yield is 100 basis points higher and ZROZ's about 146 basis points. Although not pure comparisons, I'm merely trying to illustrate the differential by using familiar bond ETFs.

Risk

BNDX's return outlook seems tame. That said, a backward look at risk metrics implies superior risk protection. For instance, a glance at the BNDX ETF's risk profile may surprise many, as the ETF possesses a lower standard deviation and value-at-risk than the IEF ETF, which is astonishing considering the IEF ETF's "flight to safety" properties. Thus, the possibility arises: BNDX might be suitable for those with the primary goal of lowering risk while maintaining exposure to moderate returns.

Another Aside: A Monthly Value at risk (5%) measures the maximum loss an asset would experience for 95% of its trading months. It's backward-looking yet provides valuable inferences.

Conclusion

Our analysis concludes that BNDX ETF might be valuable to investors seeking risk-reduction while maintaining some risk exposure. The vehicle has illustrated a lower standard deviation than U.S. safe havens and often presented excess returns, implying that opportunity beckons, especially considering the ETF's diversification benefits.

Having said that, we maintain a high-conviction outlook on U.S. treasuries. We aren't crazy about EU, Canadian, and Japanese bonds given the additional compression of sovereign spreads. Moreover, severed CDS spreads and qualitative analysis suggest that BNDX's key regions are poorly aligned from a risk-return perspective.

In essence, we think BNDX could be a useful inclusion in some investors' portfolios, but we aren't committing to a 'Buy Rating', instead, we've decided to place BNDX on 'Hold.'

data.title: Top 5 ETFs In 2025 & Best International ETFs To Buy
data.description:
Sign up for Premium!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole.

The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, U.S. investment adviser, or investment bank.

Daniel Snyder: Hey, everyone, Daniel Snyder. This is ETF Spotlight. Now, this week we're going to shake things up a little bit. We're going to look at the Top 5 ETFs so far this year based off of their inflows into the ETF. And so, we're going to jump through the Seeking Alpha website. We're going to look at the Quant rating system. And then we're also going to explore some of the country international ETFs and how you can use the ETF Screener here on the site to gain some exposure or put it on your watchlist if you're interested in those.

So, let's go ahead and dive on in. Now, first, I want to highlight, if you aren't aware, last year, we talked about this earlier in the ETF Spotlight season, that ETFs saw a record 2024 with inflows north of $1 trillion, and the money has not stopped just flowing into ETFs. Now, it could be for a variety of reasons. It could be, maybe somebody who doesn't feel comfortable picking individual stocks in this volatile market right now, and ETFs can be that safe space for people.

So, let's go ahead and highlight the Top 5. Coming in first place is VOO, the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF with that 3 basis point expense ratio. The Quant system is currently a Buy and the year-to-date flows have been $49 billion, which I don't know if you know this or not, but it has taken over the number one spot of assets under management, taking that throne away from the SPDR SPY ETF that everybody has known and loved for so long.

Now, coming in second place is IVV, the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF. Again, 3 basis point expense ratio. The Quant system is a Buy as well, and the year-to-date flows so far for this one have been $13.6 billion.

Now, coming in third place is SGOV, which is the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF that has an expense ratio of 9 basis points and the Quant system is a Buy rating on this ETF as well. I also do want to highlight if you haven't already, a friend of the show, John Bowman, there's his recent analysis on the SGOV. He has a Strong Buy on the stock, worth a read if you haven't done that already. So, and that has seen $13.3 billion of year-to-date flows so far.

Coming in fourth place is BIL, another taxable bond ETF here, the SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF. The expense ratio being 14 basis points on this one and the Quant system is also a Buy. And wrapping it up in fifth place is the VTI, Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF with 3 basis point expense ratio. And the Quant system is actually a Hold on this one, probably because of that Momentum Factor Grade there that you see being a C+ down from the A-.

So, I want to take a quick second and highlight the ETF Screener side of our – oh, I forgot to mention, VTI year-to-date flows $11.2 billion. Now, I want to take a second and jump over to the Market Data. I want to show everybody something really fast. If you haven't already, you can come over here and you can click on Countries in the Market Data tab on the left-hand rail here, and you'll have a list of all the countries with ETFs representing those. And somebody might be looking for international exposure right now.

So, if I click on Year-To-Date returns here and filter by that, we can see that Poland, Spain, Greece, Austria, and Germany are here at the top of the list. And actually, if you scroll down, I want to highlight this as well. United States is here towards the bottom. But as well, Argentina has had a little bit of a fall from grace this year after such an amazing year last year.

So, I just want to highlight that real fast because now what we're going to do is, we're going to scroll down here on the left-hand nav bar, and we do have that Top ETFs screen that is updated every day and this is based off of the Quant rating system. So, if you always want to keep an eye on that, that's right there, but I want to highlight the ETF Screener section.

So, we have a list of preset screeners here. And you'll notice that one of them is the Top International Equity ETFs. So, we're going to go ahead and click on that. And I want to show you that, here at the top of the list, Argentina is still ranking high, but Spain, Greece, Poland, Germany, you have options of ETFs that are rated highly by the Quant rating system right here already for you.

And the easiest thing to do is, you can add Advanced Filters, you can look at Dividends, you can look at their Holdings, say, I want to see the number of holdings in them. I want to see their performance year-to-date, which is what we were talking about and see risk or anything else, but I'm going to go ahead and click Done on that. You'll see that instantly pops up here on the right-hand side, giving me the data that I want to keep an eye on.

Now, what you can also do here, which is really great, is you can either save this screen by just clicking Save As and click that and then you'll have it under your Saved Screen section here on Seeking Alpha, or you can actually create a portfolio or a watchlist, if you will, to keep an eye on them in the future.

So, for example, I don't want to watch Argentina. It’s not probably doing that performance that I'm seeing so far this year, but I can keep an eye on Spain, Greece, Poland and Germany as well and just go ahead and throw that into a new portfolio here. And I can also have the portfolio updates into my email every single morning about what's going on within these ETFs. I can dive deeper into these ETFs if I want to explore them one by one.

I can come in, I can look at the seasonality of the ETF, I can look at the holdings. If they pay a dividend, I'll see that as well. So, a lot of cool functionality here on Seeking Alpha. So, I just wanted to highlight that. Hope everybody has a great week. Thank you for tuning into this episode and we'll see you again here soon on ETF Spotlight.

Sign up for Premium!

data.title: PayPal: Recent Weakness Looks Like A Golden Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Investment thesis

Despite a strong November-December rally, my previous bullish thesis about PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL) did not age well, as the stock is tanking in 2025. As a result, PYPL is currently almost 20% cheaper than it was on November 3. I think that this weakness is temporary because PayPal's management confidently executes on the company's growth strategy and secular trends are favorable for the entire industry. Furthermore, the stock is extremely attractively valued after the YTD dip. All in all, I reiterate my “Strong Buy” rating for PYPL.

Recent developments

PayPal shared its latest quarterly earnings on February 4, delivering a confident double beat against consensus estimates. Despite the uncertain environment and inflation still running above the Fed's targets, PYPL delivered a 4.2% YoY revenue growth. Moreover, there was a relatively wide positive adjusted EPS surprise.

There was the adjusted EPS contraction in Q4 when compared to YoY. However, it was not due to some operating issues. The unfavorable difference took place as there was a one-off $275 million non-operating loss during the quarter, compared to a non-operating gain of $113 million during Q4 2023. From the operating leverage perspective, performance was solid. The gross margin expanded YoY from 39.96% to 41.64%.

Solid operating leverage helped the company generate $1.1 billion in free cash flow [FCF] during Q4 2024. Strong FCF performance ensured improvement in the company's financial position. PayPal's financial position is robust, with the cash balance just around 10% lower compared to the total debt. In addition, there was a $1.3 billion buyback during the quarter, which reinforces the management's confidence in PayPal's future prospects.

Apart from PayPal's strong financial performance and position, there is another large positive factor. Industry trends remain very favorable for PayPal as the digital payment market is thriving. According to Precedence Research, the market is expected to deliver a robust 17% CAGR over the next decade. I think that this is a massive bullish catalyst because PYPL has been historically strong in capitalizing on secular tailwinds. Of course, there is intensifying competition in the industry as giants like Apple, Google, and Meta are expanding their footprints. Nevertheless, I think that a 17% industry CAGR will give opportunity for several players to capitalize on industry growth.

As a company that is highly committed to delivering a customer-friendly and safe experience of conducting digital payments, I think that PayPal is well-positioned to absorb positive industry trends despite competition. During the latest Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference, PayPal's management highlighted the company's firm commitment to delivering on its growth strategy. Branded checkout remains the company's main priority and there is a clear roadmap to achieve 80% global adoption of PayPal's enhanced checkout experience by 2027.

Venmo, the company's fastest-growing business of the last couple of years, is set to unlock its full monetization potential. This will be achieved by integrating Venmo into the company's broader ecosystem.

The management also reiterated their firm commitment to innovate. PayPal is investing heavily in AI to streamline its internal processes, which is also highly likely to help in creating value for shareholders and enhance customers' experiences. From the innovation perspective, the company is also leveraging data with commerce API. It is crucial as it enables merchants to personalize the shopping experience by offering customized interfaces based on shopper profiles. The management expects it to become a major differentiator in driving customer conversion and loyalty.

Last but not least, the management is also working hard on leveraging new partnerships, which will help to expand PayPal's reach. The company recently added Solana and Chainlink cryptocurrencies to its platforms. Apart from the improved reach, this move also improves the experience for customers by providing greater flexibility and choice. According to Slickcharts, Solana and Chainlink are among the top-15 largest cryptocurrencies, with a combined market cap of around $75 billion.

Valuation update

PYPL experienced a quite sharp decline in 2025 with a 27% YTD share price decrease. The valuation ratios look quite good, especially compared to PYPL's historical averages. In addition, most of the ratios are lower compared to the sector median as well. In my opinion, the biggest green flag is a low 7.8 forward Price/Cash flow ratio, which is substantially lower than both the sector median and historical average.

As usual, I started my DCF analysis with the cost of equity calculation. This one is figured out quite easily as the CAPM formula is straightforward, and all the necessary variables are easily available on the Internet. The cost of equity is 10.5%.

Since PayPal has a notable portion of debt in its capital structure, WACC will be a more suitable choice to discount the DCF model. The cost of debt was figured out using the company's TTM interest expense divided by the current outstanding total debt. As a result, the discount rate is 9.32%.

Revenue assumption for FY2026 is $35.14 billion. I think that the mid-single digit long-term revenue growth rate projected by consensus is too conservative given the projected double-digit industry growth, which I have highlighted above. Therefore, for the base-case scenario, I apply a 10% revenue CAGR.

To be conservative, I use the FCF margin adjusted for stock-based compensation [SBC]. For PYPL, the TTM FCF ex-SBC margin is 7.2%. Due to intense competition, I applied a subtle 25-bps yearly expansion of the FCF margin. The perpetual growth rate is quite conservative at 3%.

The base-case scenario suggests that the business's fair value is around $103 billion. This is 66% higher than the current market cap, indicating a massive upside potential. The margin of safety is huge because even applying a 2% perpetual growth rate combined with a 5% revenue CAGR suggests that the stock is 14% undervalued.

Risks update

We navigate an extremely uncertain environment, which weighs on the sentiment around the entire stock market. New large tariffs were introduced and then suddenly paused. I think that such a high level of uncertainty might make investors much more defensive, leading to capital rotating from growth to value. As a result, growth stocks like PYPL might suffer despite being high-quality companies.

Apart from the sentiment, there is a great level of uncertainty about the long-term effect of tariffs on the U.S. economy. Several large investment banks increased their recession probability projections. This looks like a fair move because tariffs can potentially lead to undermining the intensity of international trade. If that is the case, PayPal might suffer, as there will be less economic activity and fewer digital payment transactions overall.

In addition, PYPL has been historically quite weak in May. According to the above bar chart, the win rate in May was only 33% over the last decade. On the other hand, PYPL's performance during summer is usually robust. Therefore, investors might be better off by leaving some room to double down in case of share weakness in May.

Bottom line

To conclude, despite the uncertainty caused by tariffs, I think that PYPL remains a “Strong Buy”. The company continues executing on its growth strategy, secular trends appear to be favorable, and the stock is extremely undervalued.

data.title: Tariffs Keep The Federal Reserve Focused On Inflation, But No Change Is Expected For Interest Rates
data.description:
By James Picerno

Judging by the recent decline in the policy-sensitive US 2-year Treasury yield and Fed funds futures, market expectations continue to anticipate a rate cut, if not at next month’s policy meeting then by June. TMC Research’s Fed Funds Model also suggests that easing monetary policy is appropriate, based on current conditions. But Federal Reserve officials continue to downplay expectations that a rate cut is near. A key factor: tariffs could elevate prices.

Federal Reserve governor Chris Waller is the latest in a series of policy officials who are talking up the goal of keeping inflation stable. That task could become more challenging in the near term due to tariffs, he explained on Monday. Higher inflation implies rate hikes. But Waller also said that he expects any inflation increase triggered by tariffs will be temporary due to the countereffects of slower growth and higher unemployment.

Under the large tariff scenario, economic growth is likely to slow to a crawl and significantly raise the unemployment rate. I do expect inflation to rise significantly, but if inflation expectations remain well anchored, I also expect inflation to return to a more moderate level in 2026. Inflation could rise starting in a few months and then move back down toward our target possibly as early as by the end of this year.

He added that it’s not yet clear that rate hikes will be necessary.

The preemptive policy cuts we did last fall can allow us some time to wait and see if the hard data catch up to the soft data or vice versa and how much of the tariff will be passed through to the consumer. In such a scenario, the outlook for monetary policy might not look much different than it did before March 1.

Two recent surveys of inflation expectations suggest that consumers are expecting prices to rise, which can be a self-fulling prophecy. Notably, the initial April estimate of inflation expectations in the University of Michigan’s widely followed poll surged to 6.7%. By contrast, the New York Fed’s consumer survey is substantially lower at 3.6% for the year-ahead outlook, albeit as of March. Both numbers are well above the reported 2.6% consumer inflation year-over-year trend through March, which in turn is moderately above the Fed’s 2% inflation target.

Waller noted that he places more trust in the Treasury market’s implied inflation forecast for expectations. The 5-year breakeven inflation rate, based on the nominal 5-year yield less its 5-year counterpart, was 2.31% on Monday (Apr. 15), which is roughly in line with the pre-tariff turmoil.

TMC Research’s Fed funds model shows a widening spread between the current median Fed funds target rate and the falling estimate of a neutral rate - a sign that Fed policy may be passively shifting to a more restrictive phase lately, albeit on the margins so far.

What could change the calculus and move Waller (and presumably the Fed) to consider rate hikes? Stronger growth in the labor market could be a trigger.

Meantime, the Fed appears to be watching and waiting for more data and allowing the current policy stance to continue for the near term. The wild card is still tariffs, and these are still early days. For now, the central bank seems to be comfortable with maintaining its current 4.25-4.50% Fed funds target range until inflation and/or the labor market data provides a compelling reason to change tack.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Tariffs Increase To 245% On China: What's The Limit?
data.description:
When tariffs on China were around 50%, I thought they were already very high and I never expected that after a few weeks they would reach 245%. It's a huge figure, and just as it has done so far, China will probably respond with a similar tariff. But how far will the world's two leading powers go? Increasing tariffs by 100% is no longer a surprise, in fact, the S&P 500 futures (SPX) are down “only” -0.80%.

The position of the United States and that of China

The trade war began when President Trump announced that he would impose tariffs on his main trading partners because they were responsible for the huge trade deficit. At that stage, the stock market fell by double digits in a short period of time, only to partially recover when the tariffs were suspended for 90 days and reduced to 10% in the meantime. China was the only country that did not receive a suspension or even a temporary reduction of tariffs. On the contrary, a tit-for-tat dispute began and has now culminated in the current 245%.

Being the only country that can really stand up to the United States, China did not give in to this aggressive attitude and responded appropriately. However, as stated by Foreign Ministry spokesman Lin Jian, China doesn't want any of this:

China is not willing to fight (such a war), nor is it afraid of fighting. If the US genuinely wants to solve the problem through dialogue and negotiation, it should give up its approach of imposing extreme pressure, stop threatening and blackmailing, and engage in dialogue with the Chinese side on the basis of equality, respect and mutual benefit.

The end of the trade war depends solely on the United States, but if tariffs continue to increase by triple digits, then China will do the same. In my opinion, its behavior cannot be blamed; after all, it is the second economic power and must demonstrate a certain resilience. In fact, it is showing it: in Q1 2025, GDP grew by 5.40%, well above the 5.10% target. Logically, there were no import tariffs of 245% during this period, but Sheng Laiyun, Deputy Commissioner of the NBS of China, does not believe they can seriously impact the country's long-term growth:

China’s economic foundation is stable, resilient and has great potential, so we have the courage, ability and confidence to cope with external challenges and achieve the established development goals.

Furthermore, a process of diversification is underway in terms of exports:

A diversified market pattern is taking shape, which also means that our dependence on a single export market in a certain country is declining. Therefore, China’s foreign trade is relatively resilient. The just-released first-quarter foreign trade data also shows that despite the increase in external restrictions, exports still grew by 6.9%.

In short, tariffs of 245% certainly won't favor the country's economic growth, but China's future doesn't seem compromised at all. In light of these considerations, I'm reminded of Victor Gao's words a few days ago:

China is fully prepared to fight to the very end because the world is big enough that the United States is not the totality of the market in the world. So if the United States wants to go in that direction of completely shutting itself out of the China market, be my guest. We don’t care. China has been here for 5,000 years. Most of the time, there was no United States and we survived. And if the United States wants to bully China, we will deal with the situation without the United States, and we expect to survive for another 5,000 years.

It would seem that the United States has chosen to antagonize the wrong opponent, and China is willing to go all the way. Among other things, the different monetary policies to which the two countries are subjected could be a further point in China's favor. The United States is still struggling with sticky inflation and interest rates are still high, while China is in the midst of an expansionary fiscal and monetary policy. To recover from the real estate crisis, it is injecting liquidity into the system and will continue to do so over the next quarters. Tariffs will certainly be a burden, but in an economic context with low rates, the growth of local companies will be favored. This effect, combined with export diversification, could partially offset the enormous burden of U.S. tariffs.

But a trade war isn't just about import tariffs, it can evolve into something more devious, such as knocking out the best companies in the respective countries. We have already reached this stage: China has restricted imports of Boeing aircraft, and the United States has restricted exports of the most powerful Nvidia chips to China. These types of maneuvers are very dangerous for investors, as the affected stocks can quickly become falling knives. We don't know which companies will be the next to suffer the repercussions of the trade war, but I wouldn't be surprised if it were Apple and Tesla, two companies that depend on China for manufacturing and sales. We'll see, but from this point of view China still has an advantage. Preventing Chinese tech giants from selling in the U.S. wouldn't affect their finances too much, as they mainly sell to the east, but the situation is different in reverse.

No one wins in trade wars, so it's best to end them as soon as possible. In any case, in this U.S.-China confrontation, I believe that the latter has an advantage and can afford to stand up to the aggressive increase in tariffs. Whether it's 245%, 500% or 1000%, China will continue to fight back because it has the leverage to do so; on the other hand, I'm not sure the U.S. can really sustain this situation for more than a few quarters.

Actually, I believe that if China wanted to, it could end this war in a relatively short time, as it has the greatest advantage of all on its side: a virtual monopoly on rare earths. By preventing exports to the U.S., it would lose a lot of money, but it would completely inhibit American economic growth; no one would benefit, but China would probably come out less weakened. Rare earths are the basis of all major technological devices, renewable energy, weapons and defense. Not having them means giving up all this and not being able to continue exploiting the current technological revolution.

The United States may have trillions of dollars to spend on AI CapEx, but without rare earths elements (REEs) they are going nowhere, and China accounts for 99% of the world's heavy REEs processing. It's a bit like knowing how to make the best pizza in the world but not having the flour.

Among China's latest responses, in addition to raising tariffs, the Chinese Ministry of Commerce has imposed export restrictions on 7 out of 17 rare earth elements (REEs) to the U.S. This is a clear signal that China is not afraid to use this leverage and is willing to go further in the event of a further increase in tariffs. At the moment no country in the world can replace China in the processing of REEs (and none is close to doing so), so in my opinion the U.S. must act cautiously from now on.

To go back to the title, so what is the limit of this trade war? In my opinion it lies in China's willingness to further reduce REEs exports to the U.S. It is at that point that perhaps the tones will be more diplomatic.
